name: Malawi
tagline: From the Maravi Confederacy on the shores of Lake Nyasa and the Chongoni rock-painters to the slave caravans, Scottish missions, the Chilembwe rising, and independence under Kamuzu Banda.
summary: Malawi takes both its name and its heart from Lake Malawi (Lake Nyasa), the great body of water along which the Maravi (Malawi) Confederacy of the Chewa-speaking peoples rose around the late fifteenth century. Bantu-speaking farmers had settled the Malawi plateau over the preceding centuries, and the women of the Chewa left a living tradition of rock painting at Chongoni, now a UNESCO World Heritage site. In the nineteenth century the Yao, Tumbuka and the Ngoni — refugees of the southern African Mfecane — reshaped the region, and a brutal Swahili-Arab and Yao slave trade scarred the lake. The explorer David Livingstone reached Lake Nyasa in 1859, and the Scottish Presbyterian missions he inspired at Livingstonia and Blantyre campaigned against the trade and trained an African elite. Britain proclaimed the British Central Africa Protectorate in 1891, renamed Nyasaland in 1907. John Chilembwe's short-lived 1915 rising against colonial rule and forced labour made him an early nationalist martyr, and African opposition to the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland (1953–1963) fuelled the independence movement led by Hastings Kamuzu Banda. Malawi became independent of the United Kingdom on 6 July 1964; Banda ruled as autocratic life-president until a 1993 referendum and the 1994 multiparty elections opened a democratic era of contested, sometimes turbulent, but repeatedly peaceful transfers of power.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: On 6 July 1964 the British protectorate of Nyasaland became the independent state of Malawi, a member of the Commonwealth, with Hastings Kamuzu Banda as its first prime minister. The name 'Malawi' was deliberately chosen to evoke the Maravi (Malawi) Confederacy, the Chewa-led state that had flourished on the shores of Lake Nyasa from roughly the late fifteenth century, and the lake itself was renamed Lake Malawi. The United States recognised the new nation the same day by elevating its consulate at Blantyre to embassy status. Independence was the culmination of a long struggle: the Chilembwe rising of 1915, the founding of the Nyasaland African Congress in 1944, and above all the fierce African opposition to the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland, dissolved at the end of 1963. Two years later, in 1966, Malawi became a republic with Banda as president, and in 1971 he had himself declared president for life.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From the United Kingdom on 6 July 1964; republic from 6 July 1966

quickFacts.label: Name
quickFacts.value: From the Maravi (Malawi) Confederacy, the Chewa state on Lake Nyasa, c. late 15th century

quickFacts.label: First leader
quickFacts.value: Hastings Kamuzu Banda, prime minister then president; life-president from 1971 to 1994

quickFacts.label: Colonial era
quickFacts.value: British Central Africa Protectorate from 1891; renamed Nyasaland in 1907

quickFacts.label: World Heritage
quickFacts.value: Chongoni Rock-Art Area, inscribed by UNESCO in 2006; Lake Malawi National Park, 1984

quickFacts.label: Government
quickFacts.value: Multiparty democracy since the 1993 referendum and 1994 elections; repeated peaceful alternation of power

eras.title: Bantu farmers and the rock-painters of Chongoni
eras.standfirst: Over the first centuries of the Common Era Bantu-speaking farmers settled the Malawi plateau around the great lake, where earlier hunter-gatherers and later Chewa women left an enduring tradition of rock painting at Chongoni.
body: The lands around Lake Malawi, which Europeans would call Lake Nyasa, have been inhabited for tens of thousands of years; fossil and archaeological finds place early humans near the lake far back into the Stone Age. The forested granite hills of the central plateau preserve one of the richest concentrations of rock art in Central Africa. The earliest paintings are attributed to BaTwa hunter-gatherers, while a later, distinctive tradition belongs to the Chewa farming communities — much of it the work of women, bound up with girls' initiation, funeral rites and rainmaking ceremonies that survive in living memory.

During roughly the first four centuries of the Common Era, Bantu-speaking peoples moved into the region, bringing ironworking and a settled, slash-and-burn farming of millet and sorghum. These migrations, part of the wider expansion of Bantu-speakers across Central and Southern Africa, gradually displaced or absorbed earlier hunter-gatherer populations and laid the human foundations of the area's later kingdoms. The newcomers organised into clans and chiefdoms across the plateau and the lakeshore.

The Chongoni Rock-Art Area, in present-day Dedza District, gathers more than a hundred and twenty painted sites and was inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage site in 2006 for the density of its rock art and its continuing link to Chewa society. Unusually among the world's rock-painting traditions, the Chongoni paintings are tied to ceremonies still practised, including the secret nyau masking societies of the Chewa — a rare case of rock art whose meaning remains alive within the community that made it.

events.title: Bantu-speaking farmers settle the plateau
events.summary: Bantu-speaking peoples moved into the Lake Malawi region during the early centuries of the Common Era, introducing ironworking and settled agriculture and absorbing or displacing earlier hunter-gatherer communities.

events.title: The Chewa rock-painting tradition at Chongoni
events.summary: Chewa farming communities, especially women, developed a distinctive rock-painting tradition on the granite hills of the central plateau, linked to initiation, funerals and rainmaking, building on far older paintings by BaTwa hunter-gatherers.

pullquote.text: At Chongoni the paintings are not relics of a vanished world but part of ceremonies the Chewa still perform — rock art whose meaning never died.

eras.title: The Maravi Confederacy
eras.standfirst: From the late fifteenth century the Chewa-led Maravi (Malawi) Confederacy, ruled by the Kalonga, spread out from the southwestern shores of Lake Nyasa — the state from which the modern country takes its name.
body: Around 1480 the Amaravi — ancestors of the modern Chewa — established a confederacy on the southwestern shores of Lake Nyasa, having migrated, by tradition, from the region of the Congo basin. The state was headed by a paramount king, the Kalonga (also rendered Karonga), who ruled from a headquarters at Mankhamba, with subordinate chiefs drawn from the leading clans. The country's name, Malawi, derives from this Maravi state — a word often linked to the flames or rays reflected on the surface of the great lake.

At its height in the seventeenth century the Maravi Confederacy controlled a wide territory stretching from north of the Zambezi to the Dwangwa River, west toward the Luangwa, and east to the coast of present-day Mozambique, encompassing much of modern Malawi together with parts of Mozambique and Zambia. Its economy rested on millet and sorghum agriculture, fishing on the lake, ironworking and cotton cloth, and on long-distance trade. Through coastal contacts in Mozambique the Maravi exchanged ivory and iron with Portuguese and Swahili merchants, and the first European account of the region was left by the Portuguese traveller Gaspar Bocarro, who crossed it in 1616.

The confederacy began to fracture in the early eighteenth century. Quarrels among the sub-chiefs loosened the Kalonga's authority, and the growing trade in enslaved people, drawing in coastal merchants, set communities against one another. By the time new peoples arrived in the nineteenth century, the once-extensive Maravi state had broken into a patchwork of smaller Chewa chiefdoms — but its name, and its cultural imprint, endured.

events.title: Founding of the Maravi Confederacy
events.summary: The Amaravi, ancestors of the Chewa, established a confederacy under the Kalonga on the southwestern shores of Lake Nyasa, ruling from Mankhamba — the state that gave Malawi its name.

events.title: Gaspar Bocarro crosses the region
events.summary: The Portuguese traveller Gaspar Bocarro journeyed through the territory, leaving what is generally regarded as the first European account of the land and peoples of the future Malawi.

events.title: The Maravi Empire at its peak
events.summary: In the seventeenth century the confederacy administered a broad domain reaching across parts of modern Malawi, Mozambique and Zambia, trading ivory and iron with Portuguese and Swahili merchants on the coast.

events.title: Decline of the confederacy
events.summary: Fighting among the sub-chiefs and the spreading slave trade eroded the Kalonga's authority, and the Maravi state fragmented into smaller Chewa chiefdoms.

pullquote.text: From the Maravi state on the lake the country drew its name — a word often read as the flames or rays mirrored on the waters of Nyasa.
pullquote.attribution: On the origin of the name Malawi

eras.title: Migrations, missionaries and the slave trade
eras.standfirst: The nineteenth century brought the Yao, the Ngoni and the Tumbuka, a devastating Swahili-Arab and Yao slave trade on the lake, and the explorer David Livingstone, whose example planted the Scottish Presbyterian missions that fought the trade.
body: Between roughly 1820 and 1860 the human map of the lake region was redrawn. The Ngoni, armed and disciplined warrior bands uprooted by the Mfecane — the upheavals that radiated out from the rise of the Zulu kingdom in southern Africa — drove north and settled in several areas around Lake Nyasa, absorbing local peoples into their militarised society. From the east the Yao migrated in from northern Mozambique, and the Tumbuka and other groups settled the northern highlands. These movements layered new peoples and polities over the older Chewa chiefdoms.

These decades were also scarred by an intensifying trade in enslaved human beings. Swahili-Arab merchants from the East African coast pushed inland, and around 1840 the slave-trader Salim bin Abdallah (the Jumbe) established himself at Nkhotakota on the western lakeshore, which became a notorious crossing point for caravans of captives bound for the coast and the markets of Zanzibar; Islam took root there in the 1860s. The Yao, well placed astride the routes, became major participants, raiding and purchasing captives among the Chewa and others. This traffic — to be understood soberly as a catastrophe of immense human suffering — depopulated districts and fed the violence that had already weakened the Maravi state.

In 1859 the Scottish explorer and missionary David Livingstone, then leading the Zambezi expedition as a British consul charged with promoting commerce and 'the extinction of the slave-trade', reached Lake Nyasa, which he reported as a highway of the slave caravans. His call for 'Commerce, Christianity and Civilization' to end the trade inspired the founding of the Universities' Mission to Central Africa in 1860 and, after his death, the Free Church of Scotland's Livingstonia mission in 1875 and the Church of Scotland's Blantyre mission in 1876. The missions documented and denounced the slave raids, sheltered freed captives, and educated an African Christian elite that would shape the country's future.

events.title: Ngoni and Yao migrations reshape the region
events.summary: Ngoni warrior bands fleeing the Mfecane settled around Lake Nyasa, while the Yao migrated from northern Mozambique and the Tumbuka occupied the northern highlands, overlaying new peoples on the older Chewa chiefdoms.

events.title: The Jumbe and the slave trade at Nkhotakota
events.summary: The Swahili-Arab trader Salim bin Abdallah, the Jumbe, established himself at Nkhotakota, which became a major slaving entrepôt on the western lakeshore; the Yao became leading participants in the trade across the lake.

events.title: Livingstone reaches Lake Nyasa
events.summary: David Livingstone, on the Zambezi expedition, reached Lake Nyasa and reported it as a corridor of the slave caravans, calling for commerce and Christianity to end the trade.

events.title: Scottish missions founded at Livingstonia and Blantyre
events.summary: Inspired by Livingstone, the Free Church of Scotland established the Livingstonia mission in 1875 and the Church of Scotland the Blantyre mission in 1876; both campaigned against the slave trade and trained an African Christian elite.

figures.name: David Livingstone
figures.blurb: Reached Lake Nyasa in 1859 and exposed the Central African slave trade to British opinion; his call for 'Commerce, Christianity and Civilization' to end the trade inspired the Scottish missions at Livingstonia and Blantyre.

pullquote.text: Livingstone reported the lake as a highway of the slave caravans — and from his example came the missions that documented the raids and sheltered the freed.

eras.title: Protectorate, Nyasaland and the Chilembwe rising
eras.standfirst: Britain proclaimed the British Central Africa Protectorate in 1891, renamed Nyasaland in 1907; in 1915 the minister John Chilembwe led a short-lived rising against colonial rule and forced labour, becoming an early nationalist martyr.
body: British control hardened in the wake of the missions and amid the European scramble for Africa. In 1891 the British Foreign Office endorsed the proclamation of the British Central Africa Protectorate over the lands around the lake and the Shire highlands, with Harry (later Sir Harry) Johnston as its first commissioner and consul-general. Johnston suppressed the surviving slave-trading networks by force, issued certificates of claim that confirmed large blocks of land to European settlers and companies, and built the administrative skeleton of the new territory. In 1907 the protectorate was renamed Nyasaland.

Colonial rule brought European-owned estates, labour migration to the mines of Southern Rhodesia and South Africa, and the deeply resented system of thangata — labour rent demanded of Africans living on settler land. On the A. L. Bruce estate at Magomero the manager William Jervis Livingstone became a particular focus of grievance for his harsh enforcement of these obligations and his treatment of African tenants and churches.

John Chilembwe, an American-educated Baptist minister who had founded the independent Providence Industrial Mission at Mbombwe in 1900, channelled these grievances — sharpened by the conscription of Africans into the First World War — into open revolt. On the night of 23 January 1915 his followers attacked the Bruce estate at Magomero, killing William Jervis Livingstone and two other settlers. The British crushed the rising within days; Chilembwe was shot dead near the Mozambique border in early February. Brief and militarily hopeless, the rebellion became a landmark of African resistance, and Chilembwe is honoured today as a national hero and martyr of Malawian nationalism.

events.title: British Central Africa Protectorate proclaimed
events.summary: Britain proclaimed a protectorate over the lake region and the Shire highlands, with Harry Johnston as first commissioner; he suppressed the slave trade by force and parcelled out land to European settlers through certificates of claim.

events.title: The protectorate renamed Nyasaland
events.summary: The British Central Africa Protectorate was renamed the Nyasaland Protectorate, the name by which the territory would be known until independence.

events.title: The Chilembwe rising
events.summary: Reverend John Chilembwe led an uprising against colonial rule, forced labour and wartime conscription; his followers attacked the A. L. Bruce estate at Magomero and killed William Jervis Livingstone before British forces swiftly suppressed the revolt.

events.title: Chilembwe killed
events.summary: John Chilembwe was shot dead as he fled toward Mozambique; though the rising failed, he became an enduring symbol of Malawian nationalism and is honoured as a national martyr.

events.title: Founding of the Nyasaland African Congress
events.summary: African political associations coalesced into the Nyasaland African Congress, the first territory-wide nationalist body, which would later spearhead opposition to federation.

figures.name: John Chilembwe
figures.blurb: American-educated founder of the Providence Industrial Mission who led the 1915 rising against colonial rule, forced labour and wartime conscription; killed within weeks, he is revered as an early martyr of Malawian nationalism.

pullquote.text: The rising lasted only days and could never have succeeded — yet Chilembwe's name became a banner for the nationalism that followed.

eras.title: Federation, Banda and independence
eras.standfirst: African opposition to the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland (1953–1963) galvanised the independence movement under Hastings Kamuzu Banda; Malawi became independent on 6 July 1964, and by 1971 Banda had made himself president for life.
body: In 1953 Britain joined Nyasaland with Southern and Northern Rhodesia in the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland, a settler-dominated union that Africans across Nyasaland fiercely opposed, fearing permanent rule by the white minority of the south. Resistance crystallised around the Nyasaland African Congress, and in 1958 the British-trained physician Hastings Kamuzu Banda, long abroad, returned to lead it. Agitation against the Federation mounted, and in March 1959 the colonial governor declared a state of emergency, detaining Banda and over a thousand Congress supporters in 'Operation Sunrise'; a British commission of inquiry under Justice Devlin later described Nyasaland as, in effect, a 'police state'.

Released in 1960, Banda led the renamed Malawi Congress Party to a sweeping victory in the 1961 legislative elections. African opposition, together with the federation's internal strains, doomed the union, which was dissolved at the end of 1963. Banda became prime minister of self-governing Nyasaland in February 1963, and on 6 July 1964 the territory became the fully independent state of Malawi within the Commonwealth, the name reviving that of the old Maravi Confederacy.

Independence quickly gave way to one-man rule. Within months a 'cabinet crisis' saw Banda dismiss or alienate his younger, more radical ministers, several of whom fled into exile. In 1966 Malawi became a republic with Banda as executive president and the Malawi Congress Party as the sole legal party, and in 1971 the National Assembly declared him President for Life. Malawi had become a one-party state under the unchallengeable authority of Kamuzu Banda.

events.title: Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland created
events.summary: Britain bound Nyasaland into a settler-dominated federation with Southern and Northern Rhodesia, provoking deep and sustained African opposition across Nyasaland.

events.title: State of emergency and Operation Sunrise
events.summary: Amid mounting agitation against federation, the governor declared a state of emergency and detained Banda and over a thousand Congress members; the Devlin Commission later condemned the use of force and likened Nyasaland to a police state.

events.title: Malawi Congress Party wins the elections
events.summary: Banda's Malawi Congress Party won the 1961 legislative elections decisively, securing African control of the colonial legislature and accelerating the path to self-government.

events.title: The Federation dissolved
events.summary: Faced with African opposition and irreconcilable tensions, the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland was dissolved at the end of 1963, clearing the way for the separate independence of Malawi, Zambia and Rhodesia.

events.title: Malawi independent
events.summary: Nyasaland became the independent state of Malawi within the Commonwealth, with Hastings Kamuzu Banda as prime minister; the United States recognised the new nation the same day.

events.title: Banda declared President for Life
events.summary: After Malawi became a republic in 1966 with the Malawi Congress Party as sole legal party, the National Assembly in 1971 proclaimed Banda President for Life, completing the one-party state.

pullquote.text: A British commission of inquiry concluded that Nyasaland had become, for the moment, a police state.

eras.title: The Banda regime and the democratic transition
eras.standfirst: Hastings Banda ruled Malawi as an austere life-president for three decades; a 1993 referendum and the 1994 multiparty elections ended his regime and opened a democratic era of contested but repeatedly peaceful transfers of power.
body: For some thirty years Banda governed Malawi as a strict, paternalistic and repressive autocrat. The Malawi Congress Party penetrated every level of society; opponents were detained, exiled, or in some cases killed, and the press, dress, and even literature were tightly controlled. Banda pursued a conservative, pro-Western foreign policy that included, controversially, diplomatic ties with apartheid South Africa, while at home a small circle around him dominated the economy. Behind a façade of stability lay one of post-colonial Africa's more rigidly authoritarian states.

By the early 1990s pressure for change mounted from churches, exiles, donors and a restive population. In a referendum on 14 June 1993 Malawians voted decisively — about two to one — to abandon the one-party system. A transitional constitution followed, and in the first multiparty general election on 17 May 1994 Banda was defeated. Bakili Muluzi of the United Democratic Front won the presidency, and the aged Banda peacefully relinquished power, dying in 1997. Malawi had completed a remarkably orderly transition from dictatorship to democracy.

Multiparty Malawi has since seen competitive, frequently turbulent politics, but also repeated peaceful changes of leader. Bingu wa Mutharika won the presidency in 2004; after his death in office in 2012 his vice-president Joyce Banda became the country's first woman head of state; Peter Mutharika won in 2014. In a landmark moment in 2020, the Constitutional Court annulled a flawed 2019 election and ordered a re-run, which the opposition's Lazarus Chakwera won — a rare judicial overturning of a presidential result in Africa. In the 2025 general election Peter Mutharika returned to office, defeating the incumbent Chakwera, who conceded — another peaceful alternation in one of the continent's more resilient democracies, even as Malawi remains among the world's poorest countries.

events.title: Three decades of one-party rule
events.summary: Banda ruled as life-president through the Malawi Congress Party, detaining, exiling or killing opponents and maintaining a tightly controlled, conservative and pro-Western state.

events.title: Referendum ends the one-party state
events.summary: Malawians voted by roughly two to one to abandon single-party rule and adopt multiparty democracy, opening the way to free elections.

events.title: First multiparty elections; Banda defeated
events.summary: In the first multiparty general election since independence, Bakili Muluzi of the United Democratic Front defeated Hastings Banda, who peacefully gave up power after three decades.

events.title: Joyce Banda becomes head of state
events.summary: After the death in office of President Bingu wa Mutharika, Vice-President Joyce Banda succeeded him, becoming Malawi's first woman president.

events.title: Court-ordered election re-run
events.summary: After the Constitutional Court annulled the 2019 presidential election for irregularities, Lazarus Chakwera won the court-ordered re-run, a rare judicial reversal of a presidential result on the continent.

events.title: Peaceful alternation of power
events.summary: Peter Mutharika won the 2025 general election, defeating the incumbent Lazarus Chakwera, who conceded — marking another peaceful transfer of power in multiparty Malawi.

figures.name: Hastings Kamuzu Banda
figures.blurb: British-trained physician who led Malawi to independence in 1964 and ruled as autocratic life-president from 1971 until his defeat in the 1994 multiparty election; he relinquished power peacefully and died in 1997.

figures.name: Bakili Muluzi
figures.blurb: United Democratic Front leader who defeated Banda in the 1994 election to become Malawi's first democratically elected president of the multiparty era, serving two terms.

pullquote.text: Malawians voted by about two to one to end the one-party state — and the aged life-president stepped down without a shot.

figures.name: Hastings Kamuzu Banda
figures.blurb: Led Nyasaland to independence as Malawi on 6 July 1964 and ruled as autocratic life-president through the Malawi Congress Party until the 1994 democratic transition, which he accepted peacefully.

figures.name: John Chilembwe
figures.blurb: Baptist minister and founder of the Providence Industrial Mission whose brief 1915 rising against colonial rule and forced labour made him an enduring symbol of Malawian nationalism.

figures.name: David Livingstone
figures.blurb: Scottish missionary-explorer who reached Lake Nyasa in 1859 and whose campaign against the slave trade inspired the Scottish Presbyterian missions at Livingstonia and Blantyre.

sources.label: Formation of the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland
A Guide to the History of Recognition: Malawi
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian
